efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to brush as writer is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **writer** uses a **pen**. This pattern links a **tool or instrument** to the **person who uses it**. So who uses a **brush**?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'tool to person who uses it' analogies — think about who uses what at work!
How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to hand as helmet is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **page** is part of a **book**. In this pattern, the first word is a **small piece** of the second word. So what is a **key** part of?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'part to whole' analogies — where one thing is a piece of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: part is to whole as part is to whole (think jigsaw pieces!)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to bear as cygnet is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **puppy** is to **dog** — can you see the connection? This is one of the most common analogy patterns: **baby animal → adult animal**. Once you spot it in the first pair, just apply the same idea to *
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'young animal to adult animal' analogies — these come up loads, and some of the baby names are really surprising!
How to apply the pattern: baby is to adult as baby is to adult (once you see it, you can't miss it)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to patient as teacher is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **writer** uses a **pen**. This pattern links a **tool or instrument** to the **person who uses it**. So who uses a **brush**?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'tool to person who uses it' analogies — think about who uses what at work!
How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'part to whole' analogies — where one thing is a piece of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: part is to whole as part is to whole (think jigsaw pieces!)

lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'part to whole' analogies — where one thing is a piece of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: part is to whole as part is to whole (think jigsaw pieces!)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to sheep as pack is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **bird** lives in a **nest**. This pattern links an **animal** to its **home or dwelling**. So where does a **rabbit** live?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'animal to its home' analogies — did you know an otter's home is called a holt?
How to apply the pattern: animal is to its home as animal is to its home (there are some brilliant ones to learn!)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to tired as shiver is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **heat** causes **melt**. In this pattern, the first word **causes** or **leads to** the second. So what does **cold** cause?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'cause and effect' analogies — one thing leads to another, like dominoes!
How to apply the pattern: cause leads to effect as cause leads to effect

How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'tool to person who uses it' analogies — think about who uses what at work!
How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to uncle as niece is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Are **"hot"** and **"cold"** synonyms (words that mean the same) or antonyms (words that mean the opposite)? Decide which, then apply the same rule to **"big"**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot analogies where the relationship IS about words meaning the same (synonym) or the opposite (antonym) — two skills in one!
How to apply the pattern: word is to synonym/antonym as word is to synonym/antonym

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to fruit as carrot is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **rose** is a type of **flower**. In this pattern, the first word is a **specific example** and the second is the **group or category** it belongs to. So what group does **Mars** belong to?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'example to category' analogies — where one thing is a type of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: example is to category as example is to category (think sorting things into groups!)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to oven as gardener is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **writer** uses a **pen**. This pattern links a **tool or instrument** to the **person who uses it**. So who uses a **brush**?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'tool to person who uses it' analogies — think about who uses what at work!
How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to dog as chick is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **puppy** is to **dog** — can you see the connection? This is one of the most common analogy patterns: **baby animal → adult animal**. Once you spot it in the first pair, just apply the same idea to *
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'young animal to adult animal' analogies — these come up loads, and some of the baby names are really surprising!

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to bread as wool is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: An **author** creates a **book**. In this pattern, the first word is a **person who makes things** and the second is **what they make**. So what does a **composer** create?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'creator to creation' analogies — who makes what? Think about people and the things they create!
How to apply the pattern: creator is to creation as creator is to creation

efficacyPairs.questionText: ___ is to learn as kitchen is to ___
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: A **rose** is a type of **flower**. In this pattern, the first word is a **specific example** and the second is the **group or category** it belongs to. So what group does **Mars** belong to?
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot 'example to category' analogies — where one thing is a type of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: example is to category as example is to category (think sorting things into groups!)
